common_name: California Wonder Bell Pepper (Classic Red/Green Bell Pepper)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before the last frost date; transplant into warm, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun at or just after the last frost date; in zone 9, transplant in mid-February for spring production — peppers require consistent warmth and will stall in cool spring weather below 55°F.

directions: Set out transplants in warm, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; California Wonder is the standard classic bell pepper — introduced in 1928 and for most of the twentieth century THE bell pepper grown in American home gardens and produced commercially. The large, blocky, thick-walled, four-lobed fruits are harvested green when mature (70–80 days after transplant) or allowed to ripen to deep red for sweeter, fully ripe peppers. The thick, crisp walls and mild, sweet, distinctly 'bell pepper' flavor are the benchmarks against which other bell peppers are measured. California Wonder is a vigorous, productive plant well-suited to Gulf Coast growing conditions — tolerant of heat and humidity better than some northern-bred bell peppers. In zone 9, bell peppers can produce from February transplants through June and then resume production in September–October after the worst summer heat passes — temperatures above 90°F during flowering cause blossom drop. Apply calcium-containing fertilizer (or side-dress with gypsum) to prevent blossom end rot, which is common in bell peppers during irregular watering. The plants are technically short-lived perennials in frost-free zone 9 and can be carried over winter for a second year of production in protected locations.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before the last frost date; transplant into warm, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun at or just after the last frost date; in zone 9, transplant in mid-February for spring production and again in late July for fall production.

directions: Set out transplants in warm, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun after last frost; Poblano is the classic Mexican culinary pepper — a mildly hot (1,000–2,000 Scoville units), wide, heart-shaped to triangular pepper that is the foundational ingredient in chiles rellenos, mole negro, and dozens of traditional Mexican dishes. Harvested green (poblano) or dried red (ancho or mulato depending on variety), these peppers have a rich, complex, slightly smoky flavor with moderate heat that defines the deep, layered character of traditional Mexican cuisine. The large, thick-walled fruits reach 4–5 inches long and 2.5–3 inches wide, with deep green color maturing to red-brown. Poblano is one of the most heat-tolerant culinary peppers and performs exceptionally well in zone 9's summer temperatures — unlike many sweet bell peppers, poblano tolerates the high summer heat of the Gulf South and continues producing through the hottest months with consistent irrigation. In zone 9, the plant can become a perennial woody shrub if protected from hard freezes, producing for 2–3 years from a single planting. Dry ripe red-brown poblanos to make ancho chiles — a key ingredient in red mole — by hanging in bundles in a warm, dry, well-ventilated location for 3–4 weeks.

common_name: Ichiban Eggplant (Japanese Eggplant, Long Slender Eggplant)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting; transplant into warm, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5) soil in full sun when night temperatures remain above 55°F; in zone 9, transplant in mid-February to early March and again in late July for a productive fall crop.

directions: Set out transplants in warm, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun when night temperatures stay above 55°F; Ichiban is the classic Japanese-type slender eggplant — producing long (10–12 inch), slender (1.5–2 inch diameter), cylindrical, deep purple-black fruits that are harvested young and tender with exceptionally thin, delicate skin, very few seeds, and mild, sweet, non-bitter flesh. The thin skin and low seed content of Ichiban-type eggplants produce a fundamentally different culinary result from standard oval Italian-type eggplants — the flesh is tender-soft rather than dense, the flavor is milder and sweeter, and the skin is thin enough to be eaten without peeling. This makes Ichiban particularly well-suited to quick stir-frying, grilling, and Asian cooking methods. In zone 9, Ichiban is one of the most heat-tolerant vegetables available — eggplants love the hot, humid Gulf Coast summer that defeats many vegetables, and Ichiban's slender fruit type tends to size up and set fruit more reliably in extreme heat than standard round or oval types. Harvest when the fruit skin is deep, glossy purple-black and before any softening or browning of the calyx — once the skin loses its gloss, the fruit is overripe. The plants can be maintained year-round as perennial shrubs in frost-free zone 10–11, and in zone 9 they often survive mild winters and produce prolifically in their second year.

common_name: Pinkeye Purple Hull Cowpea (Southern Pea, Purple Hull Pea)

planting.note: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep, 3–4 inches apart in rows 24–36 inches apart after soil thoroughly warms to 65°F and all frost danger passes; in zone 9, plant from late March through late July for successive harvests — Pinkeye Purple Hull is one of the most heat-tolerant crops and thrives in the peak Gulf South summer.

directions: Direct sow in warm, well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun after soil warms to 65°F; Pinkeye Purple Hull is the most widely grown and most beloved Southern pea (cowpea) cultivar in Gulf South gardens — a semi-vining bush variety producing pods with a distinctive purple hull (pod exterior) that turns from green to vivid purple-magenta at the ideal harvest stage for fresh-shelling, making it easy to identify perfect harvest timing. The peas inside have the distinctive pink 'eye' (hilum marking) characteristic of this type, with a rich, full, starchy-creamy flavor that is the authentic taste of Southern cooking — distinctive, earthy, and satisfying in ways that fresh garden peas cannot replicate. Cowpeas are among the most heat-tolerant vegetables in cultivation and actually perform best during the hottest summer months when most other crops fail — the Gulf South summer that defeats beans, cucumbers, and squash is ideal cowpea growing weather. They fix atmospheric nitrogen through root nodule bacteria, enriching the soil for follow-on crops. For fresh eating, harvest when the majority of the pod has turned purple and feels firm and fully rounded but before the pod dries. For dried peas, allow the pods to turn tan and dry completely on the vine. The dried peas store indefinitely and are the foundation of traditional Southern New Year's black-eyed pea dishes.

common_name: Peaches and Cream Sweet Corn (Bicolor Sweet Corn)

planting.note: Direct sow 1 inch deep, 8–12 inches apart in blocks of at least 4 rows (not single long rows) for adequate pollination; in zone 9, plant in February–March for spring harvest or late July for fall harvest; sweet corn requires a large block planting for wind pollination — isolated rows produce poorly filled ears.

directions: Direct sow in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in blocks of at least 4 rows for adequate cross-pollination; Peaches and Cream is the most popular bicolor sweet corn cultivar in American home gardens — producing medium-large ears with yellow and white kernels in a random bicolor mosaic pattern that gives the variety its name and provides both sweet varieties in a single ear. The flavor is typically described as rich, sweet, and complex — the combination of white (typically sweeter) and yellow (typically more 'corny' in flavor) kernels in each bite creates a distinctive eating experience that many gardeners prefer to all-yellow or all-white varieties. Peaches and Cream is an 'SE' (sugar-enhanced) sweet corn type — sweeter than standard su sweet corn but not as sweet or tender-skinned as sh2 (supersweet) types. The SE type is more forgiving of growing conditions and holds its sweetness longer after harvest than standard sweet corn. In zone 9, sweet corn is a spring and fall crop — plant in late February for June harvest and in late July for October harvest. The hot summer is too demanding for quality corn. Corn is a heavy nitrogen feeder — side-dress with nitrogen fertilizer when plants are 12 inches tall and again when tasseling. Corn earworms are the primary pest and must be managed with Bt applications at silk emergence.
